News.title: Shocking moment US Top Gun pilot is nearly battered by pipe-wielding local after surviving Kuwait fighter jet crash
News.content:
THIS is the shocking moment a US pilot is almost battered by a pipe-wielding local after he survived a fighter jet crash in Kuwait.

Footage shows a group of men aggressively shouting as they approach the pilot, while one raises a metal pole above his head.





The pilot scrambles to detach himself from his parachute as one of the men tries to pull him forward by its chord.

As the men close in, the American can be heard shouting “Back up” repeatedly.

He then gets down on his knees with his hands outstretched in front of him.



Suddenly the pilot shouts “I’m American” and the local immediately lowers his weapon before the group retreats.

The pilot was aboard one of three US Air Force F-15E Strike Eagle jets shot down in a “friendly fire” incident over Kuwait on Monday.

Dramatic footage emerged showing the planes spiralling out of control as they plummet to the ground near the US Ali Al Salem base.

It comes as…



All pilots successfully ejected and were seen alive on the ground.

Other footage showed a female pilot smiling as a local approached her to offer his assistance.

A man recording the video can be heard saying: “You are safe. Thank you for helping us.”

While a third video showed another pilot, who appears to be able to walk, surrounded by Kuwaiti security officers.

Kuwait’s Defence Ministry confirmed several US warplanes crashed – with crews surviving.



US Central Command said in a statement: “Three U.S. F-15E Strike Eagles flying in support of Operation Epic Fury went down over Kuwait due to an apparent friendly fire incident.

“During active combat—that included attacks from Iranian aircraft, ballistic missiles, and drones — the US Air Force fighter jets were mistakenly shot down by Kuwaiti air defenses.

“All six aircrew ejected safely, have been safely recovered, and are in stable condition. Kuwait has acknowledged this incident, and we are grateful for the efforts of the Kuwaiti defense forces and their support in this ongoing operation.”

The cause of the incident is under investigation, it added.

Kuwait hosts the largest US military presence in the region, with some 13,500 troops stationed in the country, as well as Camp Arifjan, the forward HQ of US Army Central.



Iran previously claimed to be behind the attack, according to Iranian state media.

The incident came on Iran’s third day of retaliatory attacks after an American-Israeli operation took out Supreme Leader Ayatollah Ali Khamenei.

Iran spread its strikes across the major Gulf States, with explosions rocking Kuwait, Qatar and the UAE over the weekend.

US President Donald Trump has said strikes will continue until its objectives are achieved and could last up to four weeks, with the “hardest hits yet to come”.

RECOMMENDED STORIES

Americans have been ordered to stay away from the US embassy in Kuwait after smoke was seen rising from the area near the building on Monday.



As the war enters its fourth day on Tuesday, the US embassy in Saudi Arabia was blitzed in a suspected Iranian drone attack.

News.title: ‘Contaminated’ meat is urgently recalled by Tesco over salmonella fears with 48-hour isolation warning
News.content:
TESCO has recalled a meat product over fears it is contaminated with Salmonella.

Anyone experiencing symptoms is warned to stay away from work for 48 hours.



Manufactured by Sacor, Bastides Saucisson Sec is sold exclusively in Tesco stores.



Sacor is pulling the product from shelves and have issued a recall notice to alert customers that the product may contain Salmonella.

Symptoms caused by Salmonella usually include fever, diarrhoea and abdominal cramps.

The manufacturer has advised those who purchased: “If you have bought the above product do not eat it. Instead, return it to your nearest Tesco store for a full refund.”

If you experience diarrhoea or vomiting most workplaces require you to you should stay away from work, school or nursery until you have stopped having symptoms for at least 48 hours to avoid passing it on to others.

News.title: Cops release CCTV after woman ‘sexually assaulted’ by female at bus stop opposite Esso garage
News.content:
HORROR ATTACK Cops release CCTV after woman ‘sexually assaulted’ by female at bus stop opposite Esso garage

COPS have launched an urgent appeal for help finding a woman after a sexual assault in broad daylight.

Police shared a picture from CCTV footage of a woman wearing all black with dark blonde hair.



Gloucestershire Police said the sexual assault happened at a bus stop in Churchdown, Gloucester on Tuesday.

A police spokesperson said the force received a report that a woman had “approached and kissed a female on the cheek without her consent”.



They added the unknown woman then grabbed her by the arm as she tried to board a bus.

The alleged incident occurred in broad daylight, around 7.10am opposite an Esso garage in the Cotswolds village.

CCTV footage from a shop shows the woman police would like to speak to wearing black trousers and jacket, as well as a backpack.

She has her hair tied back, and appears to be holding a dog lead.



Gloucestershire Police said it wants to speak with the woman in relation to the incident.

It has launched an appeal for information about the woman pictured on CCTV in connection with the sexual assault report.

News.title: Tributes to ‘dearly loved’ teenager, 18, who died in horror car crash near market town as two seriously injured
News.content:
'GREATLY MISSED' Tributes to ‘dearly loved’ teenager, 18, who died in horror car crash near market town as two seriously injured

A DEVASTATED family have paid tribute to their teen son who sadly died in a car crash.

Dorset Police responded to a road traffic collision report in Wareham on Wednesday 18.



The incident involved a collision with a black Volvo V70 on Morden Road.



Joshua Blake, 18, was a passenger in the car and tragically declared dead at the scene.

The driver, a male in his 20s, and another passenger, a male in his teens, were also in the car.

Both sustained serious injuries and were rushed to hospital where they are currently still being treated.

In a touching tribute, Joshua’s family said: “Joshua had only just celebrated his 18th birthday before he tragically lost his life.



“He leaves behind his loving parents Martin and Caroline, brother Samuel, sister Chloe, his wider family and many friends.

“Joshua had a passion for supercars, which he always took the opportunity to photograph. He enjoyed riding his motorbike.

“He was dearly loved and will be greatly missed, always remembered by all who knew him.”

Cops from the Serious Collision Investigation Team are continuing to investigate the crash to understand the full circumstances of the incident.



Any witnesses, those with dashcam footage, or any relevant information, have been urged to come forward.

Dorset Police have asked the members of the public to contact them online, via email at scit@dorset.pnn.police.uk or by calling 101, quoting occurrence number 55260024369.

Alternatively, independent charity Crimestoppers can be contacted anonymously online using its website or by calling Freephone 0800 555 111.

News.title: The creepy ‘Girlfriend for Hire’ sites where Incels splash £50k on ‘yes women’ who promise to be ‘freaks in the sheets’
News.content:
BRUISED from the pain and bitter humiliation of rejection, a high-flying young City banker estimates he has spent more than £50,000 on his creepy solution.

The 27-year-old, from London, is one of a growing number of desperate Incels in the toxic manosphere community who are ‘hiring’ fake girlfriends, demanding subservient obedience that fuels their deeply entrenched views of resentment and anger.





The disturbing phenomenon is growing in popularity on online forums, where men are posting their ‘wish lists’ for an ideal partner and, crucially, how much they are willing to pay.

Women can make thousands of pounds by providing the ‘girlfriend experience’, which is usually conducted entirely over the internet, as a simulation of loving interaction that is not simply just about sex.

However, as a new documentary reveals, there remain troubling ethical questions over the murky business, as unstable men with mental health issues pay to assert ‘dominance’ over the women they pay.

Speaking to Ben Zand, the presenter of HUMAN: Why Men Rent Girlfriends, the banker – known only as ‘T’ – admits he has been radicalised by misogynistic influencers such as Andrew Tate, who has been accused of driving sexism in schools with his warped videos.

“I don’t want the realities of life. I want the yes woman who doesn’t say no,” T says.

“I would describe myself as a 27-year-old single male with a successful career but lacking in one department – the love department.

“For 95 per cent of my life I’ve been single.

“It’s not that I didn’t want a relationship, I was looking, but it wasn’t happening. Every time I was rejected.”



According to T, there are many advantages to hiring a girlfriend rather than forging a genuine romance – the biggest of which is that the women are more subservient.

He says: “There are so many pros and so little cons. The online girlfriend is your dream girlfriend.

“A normal girlfriend might have work, she might be doing something, she’ll say, ‘I’m tired’ which is a fair point obviously, but an online girlfriend, they don’t experience tiredness or they wouldn’t tell you.

“Ideally they’re not saying ‘No’ that much.

“Let’s say you ask them for a photo of themselves, a normal girl might say, ‘I don’t look good today, blah blah blah.’



“But a For Hire girlfriend, they’re getting paid so they’re going to do what they’ve got to do because they know you might cancel otherwise – if they’re not making you happy.

“It’s power you wouldn’t have over them in a normal relationship.”

Following their frosty initial encounter, filmmaker Ben admits: “Very quickly T started talking in a way that I was a bit uncomfortable with.



“Talking about how he felt he was exploiting women, and how he was using money to pressure them to do things, in a situation where they just couldn’t say no.”

T uses internet forums to post requests for girlfriends, specifying the age and physical requirements he demands, before asking how much they charge.

Asked what he is looking for in a partner, he creepily explains: “A bit more submissive than ordinary women might be my niche.

“With the messaging, if she doesn’t respond to me promptly then it’s not going to work out.

“If she’s at home not doing anything, then immediately. Obviously people need to be able to do other stuff like go for water, go to the toilet.



“If she doesn’t reply, I think what’s she doing? What is more important than me?

“It’s about power, it’s my way of getting what I’m due.”

In a sign of how competitive the shadowy industry has become, some ‘girlfriends for rent’ even offer a free trial, agreeing to pretend to be in a relationship for a day without payment.

But for men like T, quickly setting up a financial transaction is the ideal scenario, and the reason he has spent more than £50,000 on bogus romances so far.

He said: “You suddenly realise, I can get whatever girl I want just by paying them – isn’t that the dream?



“I’m OK with it. Experiences are worth paying for.

“Yes, it’s a lot of money but if you start regretting how you spend your money that’s not good for you.”

Many online requests reveal the depth of mental health issues some prospective male ‘customers’ are contending with.

One post reads: “I’m searching for someone that could help me with social skills.



“I really would like it to be like we are good friends. I imagine it feeling really good if we are close together.

“Depression made me feel empty.

“And I have anxiety disorders, specifically in regard to talking that I’ve had so long I didn’t develop social skills and forgot the little I had. I was also isolated for some time.”

Another post says: “My life isn’t great right now.

“I’m really lonely and depressed and sad. So I’m willing to pay the right person to talk about intimate things and/or pretend to be my friend.”



Like these posters, T says he struggles with loneliness, but admits that feeling has developed into something more sinister.

He confesses: “I’ve been drawn towards Incel culture. Watching Andrew Tate, listening for hours, and it’s going into your head.”

The incel ideology largely revolves around one thing – the ‘black pill’ – which is a world view that tells men that unless they are attractive, wealthy and dominant they are fated to a life of irrelevance where women will never date them.

The idea of genetic fate, where you are unable to find love and attract anybody, removes responsibility for finding a partner and encourages resentment and hate towards women.

Hiring a girlfriend is often seen as a simple solution to this – although it appears to come with a dark sense of entitlement.



“For a lot of guys trying to find a real relationship is incredibly difficult,” adds T. “That’s where hiring a girlfriend comes into it, it’s a good option.

“For a lot of men it’s a way to get back what is rightly theirs.

“I work hard, do all the things I’m meant to be doing, why isn’t it working?

“I’m a good citizen of the world, I deserve a girlfriend – a pretty lady to come home to, who doesn’t say no.

“I don’t want the extra stresses, the realities of life.



“I want the yes woman because it’s going to make my life better.

“My opinions and views are shaped from the world I see around me.

“I deserve a partner who’s going to love me back. If you don’t get that you feel resentment and anger against the world.

“Hiring a girlfriend actually helps tame that anger a bit.”

Many of the women are from South East Asia or South America, where men can use their economic power to gain control.



In the documentary, Ben flies to Rio de Janeiro in Brazil and meets Aime, who has worked as a girlfriend for hire since she was 18 and says the money helped her escape her abusive father.

She went on to use her earnings to fund a university degree, but being a fake girlfriend proved so lucrative that she eventually abandoned her plans to become a lawyer.

Explaining how she feels about her clients, she says: “I definitely like them but it’s in a different way. It’s not like a boyfriend.

“I fall in love with the ones who fall in love with me, because they always spend more money on me.”



If men want to meet Aime in person, she charges $800 to spend a day together, including sex.

She says: “People who hire me want me to give them attention, they want to be deceived.

“I text them in the morning asking them about their work, they hear that I love them. I think they’re really lonely.

“They can’t get their ideal girlfriend in real life who’s sexy, who’s a lady in the streets and a freak in the sheets. So they hire me to live that illusion.

“Men are increasingly looking to hire sex workers rather than to be with an ordinary woman in an ordinary date.”



She estimates 60 per cent of clients come to her because they have failed to forge real-life relationships, often because they are struggling with mental health issues including depression or low self-esteem.

But her theory is the opposite of the radical black pill ideology. She believes that the problem with her clients is not their looks, it’s their mindset.

“I feel that I have an important role in supporting them trying to embrace their pain, embrace their emptiness, even if it’s not in a perfect way, someone still cares about them,” she says.

“A lot of my clients need therapy but they won’t do it.”

RECOMMENDED STORIES

At the end of their meeting, Ben says: “I started this investigation thinking this was a story all about sex, but T is in this for something a lot darker.”



HUMAN: Why Men Rent Girlfriends is now streaming on YouTube

News.title: Best supermarket frozen chips named and they're better than McCain and BirdsEye
News.content:
Best supermarket frozen chips named and they're better than McCain and BirdsEye

The Guardian has named the best supermarket frozen chips, and it's not one of the major brands. The easy dinner staple has a permanent place in many of our freezers, but which is the best?

View 2 Images One leading supermarket has the 'best' frozen chip brand (Image: Getty)

The ultimate supermarket frozen chip has been revealed - and surprisingly, it's not from one of the household names. This teatime essential occupies a permanent spot in countless freezers across Britain, but which variety truly reigns supreme?

‌



The Guardian has declared the finest oven chips available, judging them on quality, flavour, and nutritional value. Taking the crown are Morrisons' the Best triple-cooked skin-on chips.

‌



Achieving a perfect five-star rating, these £2.85 for 750g chips were praised as "rich" with a crispy, golden coating and a fluffy centre. The publication highlighted that Morrisons' own-brand offering boasted an "excellent texture" and represented "remarkable value", crafted from British spuds with a blend of maize, rice, pea flour and dextrose.

‌



View 2 Images Morrisons oven chips have taken the top spot (Image: Getty)

The top budget option went to Tesco's home-style straight-cut oven chips. Earning three stars, these £2.65 for 950g were deemed "outstanding value".

The Guardian characterised them as "chewy yet crunchy" with a "sweet, earthy flavour". Waitrose's No 1 frozen triple-cooked chips, priced at £3.25 for 750g, secured four stars, reports the Express.

‌



These chips are "perfectly even" and "really tasty" owing to being prepared in beef dripping. The publication acknowledged that whilst they're "pricey", they remain "reasonable value".

Strong Roots proper chips, retailing at £3.75 for 750g, also earned four stars. They were hailed as "fluffy and delicious" with a "clean, simple flavour".

They're amongst the rare oven chips deemed sustainable, holding B Corp certification, which demands elevated standards of social and environmental performance, accountability, and transparency. Koffmann's classic chunky chips are "fresh-tasting" with a "homemade feel and clean flavour".

‌



They received four stars and cost £3.50 for 800g.

McCain's naked oven chips straight cut, priced at £1.98 for 900g, managed just three stars. The "nostalgic 1980s-style" chip had "decent crunch", but was "moist and smooth" rather than fluffy.

The Guardian described the stripped-back ingredient list (simply potatoes and oil) as "remarkable". Birds Eye crispy chips also achieved only three stars, retailing at £2.48 for 900g. They were labelled "moreish" with a "savoury-sweet" flavour, though they're "closer to a french fry" than a traditional chip.

Article continues below

Whilst freshly made chips are undoubtedly superior, there's no denying the appeal of chucking a bag of frozen ones from the supermarket into your trolley. The problem is, despite being marketed as a quick fix, they actually demand quite a lengthy cooking time.

Bung frozen chips in the oven and you're looking at nearly half an hour before they're ready to scoff. By the time they've defrosted enough to be edible, you've already burnt through 20 minutes, and waiting even longer for that coveted crunch feels like an eternity.

But one simple trick makes crispiest frozen chips possible. If you give the frozen chips a quick blast in the microwave before transferring them to the oven. this slashes the cooking time from 30 minutes down to a mere 10 minutes. Microwave for 1.5 minutes and this gets them partially thawed out.

News.title: Iran war: How stranded Brits are trying to flee missile and drone strikes
News.content:
Iran war: How stranded Brits are trying to flee missile and drone strikes

Emirates and Etihad are operating limited flights to bring British nationals stranded in the Middle East back to the UK, but most departures remain cancelled

View 3 Images Iran has launched numerous strikes against targets in the Middle East (Image: UGC/UNKNOWN/AFP via Getty Images)

Desperate stranded Brits are still trying to flee the Middle East amid a barrage of missile and drone strikes ordered by the remnants of Iran's faltering leadership.

‌



A smattering of flights are set to ferry stranded Brits from the Middle East back to Blighty on Tuesday, though the lion's share of departures from the region remain grounded. Emirates has scheduled two flights bound for Heathrow and one each for Gatwick and Manchester.

‌



A number of these flights will be serviced by the airline's mammoth A380 jets, capable of accommodating up to 615 passengers depending on their configuration. Typically, Emirates operates a whopping 146 weekly flights to the UK, equating to roughly 21 per day.

‌



Ensure our latest headlines always appear at the top of your Google Search by making us a Preferred Source. Click here to activate or add us as your Preferred Source in your Google search settings.

View 3 Images Jeff and Rebecca Moses from Manchester managed to get out of Abu Dhabi (Image: PA)

How Iran missiles can hit Europe amid fears terror cells ready to unleash chaos READ MORE :

Etihad Airways has pencilled in a single UK-bound flight on Tuesday, departing from Abu Dhabi and landing at Heathrow. This journey will be undertaken by a Boeing 787-10 Dreamliner, boasting 336 passenger seats.

‌



Meanwhile, Qatar Airways - which ordinarily shuttles passengers between Qatar and both Heathrow and Gatwick - announced on Tuesday morning that its operations remain on hold due to the closure of Qatari airspace.

British Airways has scrapped its scheduled flights to Heathrow from Abu Dhabi, Amman, Bahrain, Doha and Dubai on Tuesday. The airline reassured passengers: "We are closely monitoring the situation and have cancelled a number of our flights to the Middle East. Safety is always our top priority and we would never operate a flight unless it was safe to do so."

View 3 Images Brits fled the Middle East amid Iranian strikes (Image: Joseph Hughes / SWNS)

Article continues below

Passengers booked on certain routes up until March 15 are being permitted to reschedule their flights to any date on or before March 29.

Virgin Atlantic also pulled the plug on a flight from Dubai to Heathrow. The ongoing conflict between Iran and the US and its allies has triggered extensive airspace closures in the Middle East, sparking major disruption to flights.

Approximately half a million travellers pass through airports in Dubai, Doha or Abu Dhabi daily - each are crucial transit points for journeys connecting Europe with Asia and Australia. Clearing the backlog of stranded passengers is expected to take several days.

News.title: Martin Lewis explains how to slash energy bills before new price cap
News.content:
Martin Lewis explains how to slash energy bills before new price cap

Money Saving Expert Martin Lewis has shared advice on heating efficiency and fixed tariff deals as the Ofgem energy price cap drops from £1,758 to £1,641 for average households from April 1

View 3 Images Martin Lewis has given people advice about heating bills (Image: ITV)

Millions of households are poised to see their energy bills tumble this spring after Ofgem unveiled its latest price cap, with Martin Lewis offering guidance on how families should react.

‌



From April 1, the price cap will fall from £1,758 to £1,641 for typical households - representing a 7% drop, or £117 saving. That said, what you actually pay depends entirely on how much gas and electricity you use.

‌



Chancellor Rachel Reeves announced £150 would be knocked off energy bills from April through scrapping the Energy Company Obligation and Renewables Obligation.

‌



Despite this welcome reduction, Martin Lewis has urged households they could still trim costs further by jumping to a fixed tariff deal. The Ofgem price cap doesn't put a limit on your overall energy spending - instead, it sets the maximum price you're charged per unit, plus standing charges, reports the Express.

View 3 Images The energy price cap will change again in July (Image: Getty)

Standing charges are a daily fixed cost for being connected to the grid. Therefore, your bill is still dictated by how much energy you actually consume, which means it could land higher or lower than the headline price cap amount.

‌



The price cap figure reflects what average bill payers might expect to shell out, calculated using Ofgem's projection of typical household energy usage. Looking ahead to early 2026, Martin Lewis has urged households to lock in their energy tariff with a fixed deal sitting 10-15% beneath the current price cap.

He suggested that securing a fixed energy tariff now generally makes sense if you can locate a deal 10-15% under the existing Price Cap. These price caps are expected to drop again come April "in many cases more than the Price Cap, that differential will remain," according to the Money Saving Expert.

Since the optimal price varies depending on consumption levels and whereabouts, Lewis suggests using a comparison platform like Cheap Energy Club.

‌



For those with lower consumption, EDF offers a Price Cap tracker matching the standard rate, but knocking £100 off Standing Charges annually (and through MSE you can also pocket £70 cashback). Additionally, there are EV tariffs and advanced time-of-use tariffs worth investigating.

View 3 Images Martin Lewis advises getting a fixed tariff (Image: ITV)

Heating typically accounts for the bulk of energy consumption in British homes, according to The Electrical Outlets. So with unpredictable weather continuing, Martin Lewis has delivered his final verdict on whether it's preferable to maintain heating on low constantly or switch it on only when required.

‌



The conclusion seems to be that "leaving the heating on all day leads to greater heat loss and means higher cost", per MSE.

That said, both perspectives in the heating debate carry some validity - with scope for variation depending on individual circumstances.

The Energy Saving Trust maintains running heating continuously proves less economical. It recommends the superior strategy is heating your property only when needed - guaranteeing you're exclusively spending when it's truly essential.

‌



Utilising a programmable thermostat represents the most effective way of achieving your desired home temperature at the times you require it.

That said, Heat Geek advises that if you possess a contemporary boiler or heat pump, remain at home throughout the day, or spend the majority of evenings and weekends at your residence, the 'low and slow' technique proves most effective.

Heat pumps, a low-carbon variety of electric heating, draw warmth from outdoors, whilst modern condensing boilers reclaim some waste heat before it escapes - these boilers were fitted post-2005.

‌



In these circumstances, maintaining your property at a steady 18 or 19°C may prove more energy-efficient.

Lower temperatures and gentler heating response - meaning you resist the temptation to whack up your heating when cold - enables the boiler to recapture more waste heat.

Mind you, this might not prove the wisest tactic if you're frequently away from home or if your walls comprise plasterboard (which warms up more quickly).

Article continues below

However, if you've got cavity wall and loft insulation or underfloor heating, the 'low and slow' method is perfect.

Some argue that the benefit of 'low and slow' is that it stops condensation from accumulating inside the walls each time the heating switches off, which can sap warmth from your home and potentially encourage damp and mould. Given there's no clear-cut solution, MSE recommends a trial and error approach - measuring energy usage with both methods.

Note down meter readings at the beginning and end of each trial and contrast them - though attempt to ensure the temperatures during the weeks you're evaluating are comparable.

News.title: Greggs issues update on store openings as annual profits slump
News.content:
Greggs issues update on store openings as annual profits slump

Bakery giant Greggs warned the on-the-go eating market remained "tough" as it revealed a slowdown in sales and a fall in profit - but there was a £20million boost for staff

View 3 Images Greggs says it will open more shops on retail parks and railway stations (Image: NurPhoto via Getty Images)

Sausage roll chain Greggs has vowed to stick to its target for new store openings despite revealing a slump in annual profits.

‌



The bakery favourite said it added a net - after taking account closures - 121 branches in 2025, expanding its shop estate to 2,739 locations by the end of the year. It is aiming for around 120 further openings this year as it sticks to ambitions to grow to “significantly more than 3,000 UK shops over longer term”.

‌



Yet it came as the company reported that statutory pre-tax profits crashed by 17.9% to £167.4million for the year to December 27. Underlying profits fell 9.4% to £171.9million, with bosses not expecting an improvement on this level in 2026. Greggs blamed the fall on “increased fixed costs in respect of manufacturing, logistics and technology capacity” as well as falling sales on a volume basis on stores open at least a year. Opening more stores helped Greggs’ overall takings grow by 6.8% to £2.15billion. But those in its own store open at least increased by 2.4%.

‌



Ensure our latest headlines always appear at the top of your Google Search by making us a Preferred Source. Click here to activate or add us as your Preferred Source in your Google search settings.

View 3 Images Greggs, famous for its sausage rolls, is having to respond to growing health concerns (Image: © 2024 Bloomberg Finance LP)

And takings in shops open at least a year grew by just 1.6% in the first nine weeks of this year, the company added.

‌



While best known for being on the high street, many of Greggs new stores are elsewhere in places such as petrol forecourts, supermarkets, retail parks, hospitals and university campuses. It also opened new shops at Manchester Airport and railway stations in Leeds and Dartford as well as St Pancras railway station in London.

The chain has also been forced to respond to changing eating habits and a boom in demand for fat jabs. In its results, Greggs said: “We are seeing some emerging shifts in dietary preferences, with certain consumers seeking greater choice in areas such as increased protein, more fibre and smaller portions. We expect this will be a developing trend and are confident in our ability to evolve our range to appeal to those looking for different nutritional profiles and portion sizes when eating out of home."

The firm says it hopeful that inflationary pressure which have driven up its costs and led to price rises maybe easing. Last month saw Greggs confirmed many items on its menu went up, with the chain’s every popular sausage roll increasing by another 5p to £1.35 in most stores. A regular latte went up 10p to £2.25.

‌



View 3 Images A wax effigy of a Greggs' bake appeared on a velvet cushion in Madame Tussauds last summer (Image: Getty)

Roisin Currie, chief executive of Greggs, said: “Greggs delivered a resilient performance in 2025, growing market share, alongside continued strategic progress. Looking into 2026, easing inflationary pressures should provide some support to consumer spending and demand for convenient food-on-the-go continues to underpin the market. We remain focused on broadening access to Greggs with a strong pipeline of shop openings, exciting launches and deeper customer engagement via the Greggs app.”

Greggs also announced a boost for staff among its 33,000 strong workforce. After three months, workers can opt into the company’s sharesave scheme, enabling them to buy Greggs shares at a discount, and after six months they also become eligible for our longstanding profit share scheme. Every year, 10% of the firm’s profits are divided between staff and at the end of this month they will get £20.2million between them.

‌



Darren Shirley, an analyst at broker Shore Capital, summed the results up as "little to shout about as trading slows."

Aarin Chiekrie, equity analyst at Hargreaves Lansdown, said: "Greggs served up a strong finish to the year, with sales growth accelerating in the final quarter as customers tucked into more of its freshly baked goods.

"Despite the challenges, Greggs is working hard to build the foundations for future growth. The number of shops is set to rise from 2,739 to around 3,000 over the next few years as it looks to become more accessible to more people. Menus are being adapted to changing customer preferences, and shops are staying open later to cash in on more evening customers – the group’s fastest growing day-part. In fact, nearly 75% of its stores are now open beyond 5pm.

Article continues below

"The balance sheet is in a good position, and plenty of liquidity on hand. With cost inflation set to ease this year, guidance for underlying profits to remain broadly flat looks a touch conservative. Especially given that peak investment in building out its infrastructure for future growth has passed. As long as conditions don’t sour too much for the UK consumer, there could be more than tasty treats in store for Greggs.”

News.title: Petrol prices where you live as drivers queue at forecourts amid Iran war surge
News.content:
Drivers have been urged not to panic buy amid reports of queues forming at some fuel forecourts.

Some motorists appear to have taken nothing to chance and topped up their tank just in case.

It comes amid a spike in oil prices in the wake of the escalating conflict in the Middle East. There are concerns that the war with Iran could cripple shipments of oil and gas through the key Strait of Hormuz.

An Iranian official said his country would “set fire to anyone who tries to pass through” the stretch of water as the conflict between his country and the US and Israel continued.

Ensure our latest headlines always appear at the top of your Google Search by making us a Preferred Source. Click here to activate or add us as your Preferred Source in your Google search settings.

The price of Brent crude oil rose to $82 a barrel in early trading on Tuesday. There have been warnings that, where the cost to remain at that levels, prices may begin to rise on the forecourt.

There are reports that some garages have run out of fuel, although it is unclear whether this is to do with a spike in demand or supplies issues that are unconnected. Signs showing “sorry out of use” appeared on a forecourt in Croydon, south London. Pictures also showed long queues forming at a Costco forecourt in Liverpool.

Image: Jason Roberts /Manchester Evening News) Jason Roberts /Manchester Evening News)

Luke Bosdet, of motoring group the AA, said: “There is no need for drivers to refuel any differently than normal. Not only does it take time for cost increases to work their way through to the pump, but spring is nearly here and car fuel consumption drops – engines don’t have to work as hard as they do in the winter.”

Average petrol prices have fallen by nearly 4p per litre since the start of 2026 and some analysts predict the ongoing US and Israeli attacks on Iran could reverse that price fall.

However, prices still vary widely across the country, despite efforts by the government to rein in the margins changed by petrol retailers. Last month all petrol stations were ordered to publish their prices each day to allow drivers to find the best deals, extending what had been a voluntary industry scheme.

The data shows that the average price for a litre of petrol across the country was 131.5p this week. But in Maidstone in Kent, motorists are being charged an average of 137.9p a litre - that’’s 6.4p more than the national average adding up to £3.52 extra to fill up a standard family car.

Prices are much lower in Northern Ireland with drivers in Lisburn and Castlereagh paying over 10p a litre less than the national average. The cheapest place in England to fill up your car with petrol is at a Sainsbury’s garage in County Durham.

The Sainsbury’s garage at St Helen Auckland is charging motorists 122.9p per litre, which means it would cost just over £67.50 to fill up an average 55 litre tank.

The most expensive filling station in the United Kingdom is an Asda station at M74 Southbound, South Lanarkshire. The cost of standard petrol at this station is 163.9p, meaning it would cost a total of £90 to fill up an average family car at this forecourt.

For the owners of diesel cars, the cheapest place to fill up is the Asda garage at East March in North East Lincolnshire. The cost of a litre of standard diesel at this forecourt was 129.7p, according to prices supplied by retailers.

You can use our interactive widget to find out the price of fuel at the petrol stations near you.

The conflict has seen global oil and gas shipping rates soar, with supertanker costs in the Middle East hitting all-time highs.

Shipping through the Strait of Hormuz between Iran and Oman, which carries around one-fifth of oil consumed globally as well as large quantities of liquefied natural gas, has ground to a near halt after vessels in the area were hit as Iran retaliated to US and Israeli strikes.

AA president Edmund King said: “Obviously, some global oil distribution disruption will continue depending on the length of the conflict and issues in the Strait of Hormuz.

“Pump prices in the coming weeks will inevitably increase, possibly in the short term back up to where they were at the start of the year.”

News.title: BBC travel expert says 'register yourself' to anyone affected by Middle East war
News.content:
BBC travel expert says 'register yourself' to anyone affected by Middle East war

Thousands of British nationals affected by the ongoing conflict are urged to 'register'

View Image The FCDO has all the latest travel information for affected British nationals (Image: BBC)

An expert has shared advice for anyone affected by the ongoing conflict in the Middle East. During the BBC's Monday (March 2) episode of Morning Live, consumer champion Harry Kind shared the latest guidance he recommends, as tens of thousands of British nationals remain impacted by rapid escalations across the Gulf region.

‌



Speaking to hosts Gethin Jones and Helen Skelton, he stressed the importance of keeping up to date with the latest advice from the Foreign, Commonwealth and Development Office (FCDO). He recommended 'registering yourself' with the FCDO to ensure you're getting the most recent information, as the situation is "evolving" and "changing hour by hour".

‌



He said: "I should stress this is a constantly evolving situation. It is changing hour by hour. This is touching the lives of everyone on the planet in some way or another; some are going to be really hit by this really badly. What we know at the moment is what the government is telling us about people in the Gulf regions, so Yvette Cooper, the foreign secretary, has said that there are over 300,000 British nationals across the Gulf, as people who live there, people who are travelling.

‌



"These are places like Qatar, Bahrain, and Kuwait, and the UAE, which includes Dubai Abu Dhabi. So, there's a lot of people out there. At the moment, the kind of advice is 'do not travel there unless absolutely necessary'."

Explaining the steps people should take, Harry Kind said: "If you are in the region, register with the Foreign Office. So they have their website, the FCDO website... If you register with that, as over 100,000 Britons have already done, you'll be given updates on what will be happening in the next few days, what flights will be available when, any evacuation procedures, any advice."

Content cannot be displayed without consent

‌



Providing further updates, he said: "Generally speaking, in those regions, follow the advice of local governments, that is going to be for many people to be sheltering indoors, but this is changing constantly, and I would just really urge people to keep an eye on their information, even we're seeing this morning in Cyprus, we've had an attack suspected on an RAF base. That's not impacted the tourism industry and people out there yet, but because it's constantly evolving, we need to be constantly checking."

Register your presence with the UK government

British nationals in Bahrain, Israel, Kuwait, Palestine, Qatar and United Arab Emirates can register their presence to receive direct updates from the FCDO. The FCDO says: "You should continue to follow our travel advice for the country you are in and the advice of the local authorities."

‌



Middle East travel updates

If you are travelling anywhere in the Middle East, you should:

Monitor our travel advice for the country you are in. Read our guidance on being affected by a crisis abroad. Follow the advice from local authorities.

Recently updated travel advice

News.title: International Women's Day to honour woman who disguised herself as man for rail job
News.content:
International Women's Day to honour woman who disguised herself as man for rail job

Elizabeth Holman dressed as a navvy to work on the railways in the 1850s, and is one of the earliest known women to do the job - now she is among those being honoured at King's Cross and York stations

View 5 Images The legacies and achievements of women in rail are being recognised this International Women's Day (Image: LNER / Cover Images)

A woman who disguised herself as a man to secure work on the railway is among a group of heroines being celebrated this International Women’s Day.

‌



Elizabeth Holman, one of the earliest known women to work on the British railway, defied the social restrictions of the 1850s by dressing as a navvy to undertake dangerous and physically demanding railway construction work. She is now being recognised in two new Hollywood-style Walk of Fame installations for International Women's Day on March 8.

‌



Elizabeth will be memorialised alongside Gladys Garlick, one of the first female train guards who served during WW2 air raids, and Betty Chalmers, who joined LNER in 1937 at the age of 16 and worked in an underground shelter for two years. The trailblazers are among the women honoured in LNER’s Hollywood walk of fame style installations unveiled at London King’s Cross and York train station ahead of International Women’s Day.

‌



Ensure our latest headlines always appear at the top of your Google Search by making us a Preferred Source. Click here to activate or add us as your Preferred Source in your Google search settings.

View 5 Images The installations will be in place at London King's Cross and York train stations (Image: LNER / Cover Images)

World's longest train is over a mile long and has passengers in 100 carriages READ MORE :

The installations highlight the incredible contribution of females within the rail industry, both past and present. Modern-day railway revolutionaries are also being recognised, including train driver Helen Donagher, whose quick thinking on duty saved a passenger’s life, and Rezwana Rahman, the first LNER female driver to wear a hijab.

‌



Despite the rich history of female contribution to the railway industry, research commissioned by LNER reveals a significant perception gap. If parents asked their children to draw a train driver, three quarters of UK children would draw a man, new research has found.

View 5 Images Rezwana Rahman was the first LNER female driver to wear a hijab (Image: LNER / Cover Images)

It also revealed that 97 per cent of Brits have no idea more than 35,000 women currently work in the UK railway industry. Despite three in ten women who were surveyed saying they would consider a career in rail, 67 per cent said they were unaware of opportunities available for women within the industry.

‌



Lesley Cook, daughter of Gladys Garlick, said: “Seeing my mother’s legacy live on for her unwavering dedication to British rail is such a special moment for our family. The railway is part of our roots, with my mum as a female guard which she was so passionate about and took a huge amount of pride in, and my father being a train driver.

View 5 Images Gladys Garrick was one of the first female train guards who served during WW2 air raids (Image: LNER / Cover Images)

“It’s truly incredible to see a light shone on her story and all the other women who are receiving the recognition they deserve with this installation.”

‌



LNER has long been a leading example for gender diversity and equality in the transport industry, and it shows no signs of slowing down, Paula Bullock, the train operator’s People Director said. Its latest data report reveals it has a 44 per cent female workforce, compared to the industry benchmark of 16 per cent.

Paula added: “Gender equity in the transport industry – particularly within rail – is something we’re deeply passionate about and firmly committed to improving at LNER. By unveiling this installation, we’re not just shining a spotlight on the remarkable achievements of women, past and present, we’re also recognising there is still work to be done.”

View 5 Images Women are being honoured for their contributions to the British railways (Image: LNER / Cover Images)

Article continues below

Ellie Burrows, Network Rail’s Eastern Region Managing Director, added: “It’s inspiring to see so many remarkable women from across the rail industry celebrated through this campaign. Their stories span generations - from the pioneers who opened doors for others, to the women who played vital roles during the Second World War, to the talented colleagues shaping the railway of today and tomorrow. Each of them shows what’s possible and why representation matters."

The stars will be in place at London King’s Cross until after International Women’s Day on March 8, when they will then move into the Family Lounge, and in York railway station. People can scan the QR code on the installation which takes them to LNER.co.uk to read more about the incredible stories of these women.
